items.q: How does the Brundtland Report define sustainable development?
items.correct: Meeting present needs without compromising future generations' ability to meet theirs.
items.why: Students treat sustainability as 'no growth'. The Brundtland definition explicitly balances development AND environmental concerns - it's about INTERGENERATIONAL equity, not freezing change.

items.q: Why is atmospheric nitrogen unavailable to most plants directly?
items.correct: N2 has a strong triple bond that most organisms cannot break.
items.misconception: Because plants cannot reach high enough to access it.
items.why: Students miss the chemistry. Despite 78% of air being N2, the molecule's triple bond is energetically costly to break. Only specialised nitrogen-fixing bacteria (and lightning, industrial Haber process) can convert it to usable forms.

items.correct: The maximum population an environment can sustainably support given available resources.
items.misconception: How much an animal can physically carry.
items.random_wrong: The size a species can reach.
items.why: Students take 'carrying' literally. It's an ECOLOGICAL concept - the ceiling set by food, water, space, etc. Populations may temporarily exceed it but then crash, or oscillate around it.

items.q: Turtle excluder devices reduce bycatch in:
items.why: Correct answer: Shrimp trawl fisheries. “Pelagic longlines” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: The 2022 CBD target to protect 30% of land and sea by 2030 is known as:
items.why: It's 30x30. “Aichi 20” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: If global ocean is 360 million km² and 8% is protected, MPA area (million km²) is?
items.why: Exact answer: 28.8. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
